guidelines.title: Start light
guidelines.description: Sketch lightly with guide strokes before committing to dark, final lines. Light marks let you adjust without leaving permanent mistakes.
coachCues: Try blocking this in with lighter strokes first.
These guide lines look good — now you can commit to a darker outline.

guidelines.title: Ghost the line
guidelines.description: Hover your pen over the path you're about to draw a few times before committing. This rehearses the motion so the actual stroke is smoother.
coachCues: Before that next long line, hover the path a couple of times to plan the motion.
Notice how strokes you ghost first come out cleaner.

guidelines.title: Draw from the shoulder
guidelines.description: Long lines come out smoother when you move your whole arm rather than just your wrist. Wrist motion creates short, curved strokes; shoulder motion gives long, even ones.
coachCues: Try that long line again, but move from your shoulder rather than your wrist.
The wobble in that line is usually a sign of wrist drawing — loosen up the elbow.

guidelines.title: Build from simple shapes
guidelines.description: Build complex subjects from circles, ovals, and rectangles before refining. Big simple shapes make proportion and placement easier to fix early.
coachCues: Try blocking this in as a few simple shapes first — circles and ovals.
Nice — those underlying shapes will guide all your detail lines.

guidelines.title: Find the gesture
guidelines.description: Capture the line of action or main flow of the subject before any details. Many subjects — animals, trees, even objects — have an overall posture or curve that holds the whole thing together.
coachCues: Before adding details, see if you can find the gesture line — the overall flow or lean.
I see a strong gesture coming through here — keep that energy in the final outline.

guidelines.title: Compare as you go
guidelines.description: Frequently check the size of new parts against existing ones. It's much easier to fix proportion early than after committing.
coachCues: Quick check: how does this part's size compare to the part next to it?
The proportions feel off — try comparing this section to one you already drew.

guidelines.title: Use a unit of measure
guidelines.description: Pick one part — often the head — and use it as a unit to measure everything else. Asking 'how many heads tall is this?' is a faster proportion check than guessing.
coachCues: Try using the head as a unit — how many heads tall is the body?
Picking one part as a measuring stick will help the rest fall into place.

guidelines.title: Block big shapes before small
guidelines.description: Lay in the largest shapes first. Only add small details once the big shapes feel right. Detail on top of bad proportion just locks in the mistake.
coachCues: Hold off on the small details — let's get the big shapes right first.
The big shapes are reading well now; you can start adding finer detail.

guidelines.title: Find the axis
guidelines.description: Many subjects have a central or symmetry axis. Finding it early helps everything balance around it.
coachCues: Try sketching the central axis first — it'll help both sides feel balanced.
I notice the two sides drift apart — an axis line might bring them back together.

guidelines.title: Use confident lines
guidelines.description: One firm, committed line reads better than several overlapping tentative ones. Hesitation shows up in the drawing.
coachCues: Try that edge again as a single confident pass.
Beautiful committed line — the drawing reads more sure of itself now.

guidelines.title: Avoid hairy edges
guidelines.description: Don't redraw the same edge over and over. The eye reads many small overlapping strokes as 'hair' rather than the clean line you wanted.
coachCues: I see some hairy strokes building up here — try erasing and laying down one clean line.
Resist the urge to redraw that edge — commit to one line.

guidelines.title: Vary line weight
guidelines.description: Heavier lines on the shadow side, on contact points, and on the closer edges add depth even in pure line art.
coachCues: Try thickening the underside of these branches — it adds depth.
Where this form sits on the ground, a heavier line will help anchor it.

guidelines.title: Clean junctions
guidelines.description: Where two strokes meet, make them touch cleanly — not float past each other and not stop short of meeting.
coachCues: Two of those lines drift past each other at the corner — try landing them right at the junction.
Nice clean meeting points — they make the drawing feel deliberate.

guidelines.title: Draw the negative space
guidelines.description: The shapes between things are as informative as the things themselves. Drawing the gaps often reveals proportion errors faster than drawing the subject.
coachCues: Try squinting at the spaces between the legs — those gaps will tell you if the legs are placed right.
The negative space between the branches is uneven — that can guide your next adjustment.

guidelines.title: Notice the angles
guidelines.description: Pay attention to actual tilts and angles, not what you think the angle 'should' be. We tend to draw things more upright or symmetric than they really are.
coachCues: Check the angle of that line — is it really vertical, or is it tilted?
The tilt you have here gives the pose energy — keep it.

guidelines.title: Mind the curves
guidelines.description: Identify where a line curves gently versus where it turns sharply. Mistaking one for the other is what makes a shape feel 'off' even when proportions are right.
coachCues: This curve looks too uniform — most natural curves vary in tightness along their length.
Notice how that curve flattens out before it turns sharply at the end? Mirror that on the other side.

guidelines.title: Place thoughtfully on the page
guidelines.description: Don't drop the subject dead center every time. Consider where on the page it sits and how much breathing room it gets.
coachCues: Before you commit, consider sliding the subject off-center for a more interesting layout.
Nice placement — the empty space around the subject is part of the composition now.

guidelines.title: Show overlap
guidelines.description: Letting one form pass in front of another is the easiest way to suggest depth in line art. Stop the back form's outline cleanly where the front form covers it.
coachCues: Letting one of these overlap the other will instantly add depth.
Stop the back outline where the front shape crosses it — don't draw through.

guidelines.title: Balance detail
guidelines.description: Quiet areas help busy areas read. If you render every inch, the eye has nowhere to rest and nothing stands out.
coachCues: This area is getting busy — try leaving the next section quieter so the detail lands.
The balance between detailed and empty areas is reading really well.

guidelines.title: Suggest, don't render
guidelines.description: Imply detail with a few well-placed marks rather than drawing every feather, leaf, or hair. The viewer fills in the rest.
coachCues: A few marks here will suggest the texture — you don't need to draw every one.
Resist drawing every leaf — pick a handful and let the rest live in the silhouette.

guidelines.title: Let lines follow form
guidelines.description: The direction of a stroke can describe the surface it sits on. Curves wrapping around a sphere read as 3D; lines going against the form flatten it.
coachCues: Try curving that stroke to wrap around the form — it'll read more 3D.
Those wrapping lines are doing a lot of work to suggest the round shape.

guidelines.title: Edit with restraint
guidelines.description: Sometimes the right move is to stop, not to add another mark. Many drawings get worse in the last 10% from over-rendering.
coachCues: This is looking good — I'd be careful about adding much more.
Consider stopping here. One more pass risks muddying what's already working.

guidelines.title: Embrace imperfection
guidelines.description: Slight wobble and asymmetry give a drawing personality. Chasing perfectly clean lines often deadens the drawing.
coachCues: That little wobble actually gives the line personality — leave it.
Don't chase a perfect line — your hand-drawn quality is part of the charm.
